metadata.split_role: Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide; retained for identification and comparison.

mushroom_profile.summary: Gastroleccinum Scabrosum: check host tree needs field confirmation; Dry to slightly tacky; Whitish to grayish pores; and Scabers.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Not normally obtainable as a conventional cap-on-paper print from this gastroid form
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Retained as a comparison lead only; do not infer local presence or edible status from the legacy name.

records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only.
records.mushroom_card_note: Gastroleccinum Scabrosum — legacy comparison record; not a food target and not locally cleared.
records.field_identification: Use only as a Leccinum/scaber-stalk comparison lead. Confirm the exact host tree, cap surface and color, pore color, scaber color/pattern, and flesh staining over at least 30 minutes before applying this name. This record is not cleared as an Upper Michigan food target.

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide; retained for identification and comparison.
records.notes: Ghost Bolete: pale Leccinum of wet/boggy birch-type places; pretty, but still a Leccinum caution record.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pale northern birch-associated scaber-stalk of boggy or wet habitats, with variable slow pink or basal blue staining.
staining: Pores may bruise a little brown
Flesh may remain white or slowly turn pinkish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; northern North America, including documented Michigan and Wisconsin collections.
research_notes: MushroomExpert documents Michigan and Wisconsin collections and a range from roughly the 43rd parallel northward.
The oak-associated pale look-alike Leccinum albellum may require host-tree knowledge or microscopy for confident separation.
stem_feature: Whitish to dark scabers on pale stem

records.commonness: Occasional in suitable northern wet birch habitat
records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only.
records.mushroom_card_note: Pale northern birch scaber-stalk of bogs and wet woods; completed as an ID-only record.
records.field_identification: Pale brown to nearly white cap in wet birch habitat; creamy-white pores that bruise a little brown; whitish stem with whitish, brown, or nearly black scabers. Flesh may remain white, slowly turn pinkish, or show a little blue near the stem base.
records.safety: Recognition-only; do not eat from this app entry
records.food_value: Recognition only / not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Leccinum/scaber-stalk ID is too weak here for a food recommendation; host tree, scaber color, pore color, and staining changes must be documented.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Leccinum holopus
use_links.notes: Pale birch-associated Leccinum of northern wet habitats; described from Michigan and Wisconsin collections.

records.source_note: v4.3.208 completes the Michigan/Wisconsin-relevant identification profile; food routing remains off because this record is not being used as edible authorization.

records.culinary_uses: Some Suillus are edible, but this record is treated as niche/cautious unless host and field marks line up cleanly.
records.notes: Grayish Suillus: Suillus-type bolete needing host-tree confirmation; keep as a cautious conifer associate.

mushroom_profile.summary: Grayish Suillus: check Conifers, Pine; Grayish to brownish Suillus cap; Yellowish pores; and Glandular dots possible.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Check conifer host, cap stickiness/color, pore color, stem glandular dots, and whether ring/veil traces are present.
Suppressed from normal cards because no exact public-usable image is verified.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.mushroom_card_note: Grayish Suillus: check Conifers, Pine; Grayish to brownish Suillus cap; Yellowish pores; and Glandular dots possible.
records.field_identification: Check host association (Conifers, Pine), cap surface (Grayish to brownish Suillus cap), pore surface (Yellowish pores), stem traits (Glandular dots possible), and staining reaction (Variable / needs field confirmation). Treat this as a comparison/ID record until image and local confirmation improve.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: Occasional/niche use; texture and exact Suillus ID matter.
records.mushroom_card_note: Hollow-stalked Larch Bolete: check Tamarack / larch; Dry to slightly tacky; Yellow pores; and Glandular dots or smooth stem.
records.field_identification: Check host association (Tamarack / larch), cap surface (Dry to slightly tacky), pore surface (Yellow pores), stem traits (Glandular dots or smooth stem), and staining reaction (Unknown). Treat this as a comparison/ID record until image and local confirmation improve.

images_structured.title: Hollow-stalked Larch Bolete supporting field view

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide; retained for identification and comparison.

mushroom_profile.summary: Slender whitish to pale-brown oak-associated scaber-stalk with nonstaining flesh and olive-brown print.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; eastern North America through the upper Midwest.
research_notes: Very similar to birch-associated Leccinum holopus; host tree is a major field separator.
Microscopy may be needed when host association is uncertain.
cap_surface: Dry or slightly tacky when young
May crack in a mosaic with age

records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only.
records.mushroom_card_note: Pale oak associate; separates from pale birch ghost bolete mainly by host and, when necessary, microscopy.
records.field_identification: Slender pale scaber-stalk under oak: whitish to very pale brown cap, sometimes mosaic-cracked with age; whitish pores becoming pale brownish without bruising; pale stem with fine gray-brown scabers; white to pale yellowish flesh that does not stain.
